1: presbyterō mē epiplēxēs alla parakalei hōs patera — 'do not rebuke an elder harshly, but encourage him as a father.' Epiplēssō (strike against, censure sharply — hapax legomenon in NT) is the harshest form of verbal reproof. Parakalei (encourage/exhort) — the pastoral verb that treats the person with dignity. The familial framework for community relationships: hōs patera (as a father), hōs adelphous (as brothers), hōs mēteras (as mothers), hōs adelphas (as sisters) — maps the community onto the extended family structure that was the primary social unit of the ancient world.
2: neōteras hōs adelphas en pasē hagneia — 'younger women as sisters, with absolute purity.' Hagneia (purity, chastity) — the qualifier for the sister-relationship: the familial model is invoked precisely to define appropriate limits, not to establish emotional closeness. The word appears in v.22 as well (seauton hagnon tērei — keep yourself pure) — Timothy's personal purity is the pastoral qualification for appropriate cross-gender relationships in ministry.
3: chēras tima tas ontōs chēras — 'honor widows who are truly widows.' Tima (honor) — the same word used in v.17 for honoring well-functioning elders, and in 6:1 for slaves honoring masters; it carries both relational respect and material provision (cf. Matt 15:5-6 where timē clearly means financial support). Ontōs chēras (really/truly widows) — the adverb distinguishes those genuinely without support from those with family to care for them. The passage defines 'true widow' across vv.3-16 in terms of age, marital history, piety, and absence of family.
4: ei de tis chēra tekna ē ekgona echei, manthanetōsan prōton ton idion oikon eusebeein — 'but if a widow has children or grandchildren, let them first learn to show godliness to their own household.' Manthanetōsan (let them learn) — the subjects are the children/grandchildren, not the widow. The verb 'learn' (manthanō) for fulfilling family obligations treats it as a matter of formation and practice: caring for elderly family members is not instinctive but learned and habituated. Amoimous apodidounai tois progonois (to make repayment to their forebears) — the language of reciprocal exchange: the parents who gave life and support are owed a return of care.
5: hē de ontōs chēra kai memonōmenē ēlpiken epi theon — 'the widow who is truly alone has set her hope on God.' Memonōmenē (having been left alone, perfect passive — with lasting result) characterizes her isolation not as a character trait but as her situation. Ēlpiken epi theon (has set her hope on God, perfect tense) — the settled orientation of her hope toward God is the spiritual mark of authentic widowhood. The continuous prayer (proseuchais kai tais deēsesin nuktos kai hēmeras — prayers and petitions night and day) echoes Anna's pattern in Luke 2:37 and reflects the hagiai (holy women) of early Christian ascetic practice.
6: hē de spatalōsa zōsa tethneiken — 'but the widow who lives for pleasure is dead even while she lives.' Spatalōsa (living luxuriously, self-indulgently — from spatalaō, to live wantonly, appear also in James 5:5) describes a life organized around pleasure-seeking. The paradox zōsa tethneiken (living she has died — perfect tense) echoes the spiritual death-in-life language of Eph 2:1 (you were dead in trespasses) and the contrast of 1 Tim 6:19 (laying up treasure for the age to come as 'truly life'). Self-indulgence is death even in the biological sense of life.
7: kai tauta paraggelle hina anenklētoi ōsin — 'command these things, so that they may be without blame.' The purpose clause hina anenklētoi ōsin (that they may be blameless/without accusation) extends to the widows and their families: the community's behavior toward widows and widows' own behavior must both pass scrutiny. The verb paraggelle (command) maintains the authoritative register: these are not suggestions but community-ordering directives.
8: ei de tis tōn idiōn kai malista oikeiōn ou pronoei, tēn pistin ērnētai — 'but if someone does not provide for their own, and especially for those of their household, they have denied the faith.' Pronoei (provide for, care for in advance) — the present tense of ongoing responsibility, not occasional charity. The severe judgment 'has denied the faith' (tēn pistin ērnētai — perfect tense, with ongoing effect) ranks family neglect with apostasy: the faith that saves produces care for dependents; faith without this care is a hollow claim. Apistou cheironos (worse than an unbeliever) — even pagans generally fulfilled family obligations from honor-culture expectations; the Christian who fails at this falls below the pagan baseline.
9: chēra katalegesthō mē elatton etōn hexēkonta gegoguia — 'let a widow be enrolled if she is not less than sixty years of age.' Katalegesthō (let her be enrolled/listed — the verb of official registration) implies a formal community roll (katalogos) of widows who receive regular support. The sixty-year minimum is a practical cut-off: younger widows could remarry and should (v.14). Henos andros gynē (wife of one husband — the feminine equivalent of 'husband of one wife' for overseers) — consistent marital fidelity is the criterion, not celibacy or widowhood at the time of enrollment.
10: en ergois kalois marturouménē — 'attested by good deeds.' The enrollment criterion includes a specific catalog of good works: ei eteknotrophēsen (if she raised children), ei exenodochēsen (if she showed hospitality — received strangers), ei hagiōn podas enipsen (if she washed the feet of the saints — a specific act of humble service, cf. John 13:1-17), ei thlibomenois epērkesen (if she helped the afflicted), ei panti ergō agathō epēkolouthēsen (if she devoted herself to every good work). The list itemizes hospitality, humble service, and mercy — the visible practices that constitute a 'good works' portfolio.
11: neōteras de chēras paraitou — 'but refuse younger widows.' Paraitou (refuse, decline — present middle imperative) is decisive: younger widows are not to be enrolled. The reason: hotan gar katastrēniasōsin tou Christou gamein thelousin (for when their strong desires overcome their dedication to Christ they want to marry). Katastrēniasōsin (to become wanton against, to feel strong sensual impulses overwhelming) — hapax legomenon in NT; a vivid verb for the physical desire overriding the consecrated commitment. The problem is not marriage itself (v.14 recommends it) but breaking a prior pledge to the community.
12: echouai krima hoti tēn prōtēn pistin ēthētēsan — 'having judgment because they have rejected their first pledge.' Ēthētēsan (have set aside, nullified — aorist of atheteō) — the deliberate nullification of a commitment previously made. Prōtēn pistin (first faith/pledge) — whether this means 'faith' (loyalty to Christ as a consecrated widow) or 'pledge' (a formal commitment made upon enrollment) is debated. Either way, the 'judgment' (krima) is the self-imposed consequence of their own breach of commitment, not external condemnation.
13: hama de kai argai manthanousin perierchomonai tas oikias — 'besides, they learn to be idle, going about from house to house.' Argai (idle, unoccupied — from a-ergos, without work) echoes the ataktoi (idle ones) of 2 Thess 3:11. Perierchomonai (going about, wandering around) suggests undirected movement. The compound vice: idle + wandering produces phluaroi (gossips, chatterers) and periergoI (busybodies — again the periergazomenoi wordplay of 2 Thess 3:11) who 'say things they ought not.' The result is community disruption through idle talk.
14: boulomaioun neōteras gamein, teknogonein, oikodespotein — 'I want younger widows to marry, to have children, to manage their households.' The three infinitives are deliberate: gamein (marry), teknogonein (bear children), oikodespotein (manage/master the household — a rare compound; the oikodespotēs was the household head with full management authority). Paul's counsel reverses the enrollment-restriction: the right response for younger widows is to channel energy into family formation, not enrolled celibacy. Mēdemian aphormēn didonai tō antikeimeno loidorias charin (giving the adversary no occasion for slander) — the practical motivation: vulnerable young widows in the community were targets for criticism from outsiders.
15: ēdē gar tines exetrapēsan opisō tou Satana — 'for some have already strayed after Satan.' Exetrapēsan (have turned aside, strayed — the same verb as in 1:6 for the false teachers turning to meaningless talk) marks the outcome Paul is trying to prevent: the deviation is already happening. 'After Satan' (opisō tou Satana) — the same adversarial figure who blocked Paul in 1 Thess 2:18 and fills the role of the one who tempted (1 Thess 3:5). Straying from community order into gossip and idleness is interpreted as moving toward the adversary's sphere.
16: ei tis pistē echei chēras, eparkeitō autais — 'if any believing woman has widows in her care, let her assist them.' The switch to the feminine pistē (believing woman) from the expected pistos (believing person) indicates a specific instruction to women of means: wealthy women in the community who had widows as household dependents are to care for them privately. The goal: mē bareisthō hē ekklēsia (that the church may not be burdened) — community resources are finite and should be reserved for widows with no other source of support.
17: hoi kalōs proestōtes presbyteroi diplēs timēs axiousthōsan — 'let elders who lead well be considered worthy of double honor.' Diplēs timēs (double honor) — the word timē covers both respect and material remuneration (cf. v.3 where it means support for widows). 'Double' may mean: honor in both respects (relational and material) or twice the normal rate of support. Malista hoi kopiōntes en logō kai didaskalia (especially those who labor in preaching and teaching) — the elder who teaches is worth more community support than the elder who leads without teaching, because the labor of exposition is the more demanding task.
18: legei gar hē graphē — 'for the Scripture says.' The dual citation that follows — boun aloōnta ou phimōseis (Deut 25:4) and axios ho ergatēs tou misthou autou (Luke 10:7 / Matt 10:10) — places a dominical saying on the same level as Torah by the shared verb legei hē graphē. This is one of the earliest explicit treatments of a saying of Jesus as 'Scripture' (graphē). The hermeneutical move: the agricultural law about oxen (Deut 25:4) is applied allegorically to human workers, as Paul does explicitly in 1 Cor 9:9-12, where he says 'it is written for our sake.'
19: kata presbyterou katēgorian mē paradechou — 'do not admit an accusation against an elder except on the basis of two or three witnesses.' The two-witness rule derives from Deut 19:15 (a charge must be established by two or three witnesses) — a foundational Jewish legal principle. Its application to elders provides both protection against false accusations (elders are visible and vulnerable targets) and due process: accusations require substantiation before Timothy acts on them. The principle protects institutional stability while not immunizing leaders from accountability.
20: tous hamartanontas enōpion pantōn elegche — 'those who persist in sin, rebuke in the presence of all.' Hamartanontas (sinning — present participle, ongoing sin) distinguishes this from a past resolved failure: the rebuke is for those continuing in sin after private address has failed. Enōpion pantōn (in the presence of all) — public rebuke preserves the community's moral seriousness and creates a deterrent effect: hina kai hoi loipoi phobon echōsin (that the rest may stand in fear). The public discipline serves the entire community, not just the individual being addressed.
21: diamartyromai enōpion tou theou kai Christou Iēsou kai tōn eklektōn angelōn — 'I charge you in the presence of God and of Christ Jesus and of the elect angels.' The tripartite witness — God, Christ, angels — frames what follows as a solemn obligation performed before the ultimate audience. Eklektōn angelōn (elect/chosen angels) — a distinctive phrase, contrasting with the fallen angels who serve the adversary; the chosen angels witness and ratify the divine administration of the church. Hina tauta phylassēs chōris prokrimatos mēden poiōn kata prosklēsin (that you keep these things without prejudging, doing nothing from favoritism) — partiality and pre-judgment are the twin corruption risks in disciplinary proceedings.
22: cheiras tacheōs mēdeni epitithei mēde koinōnei hamartiais allotriois — 'do not be hasty in the laying on of hands, and do not participate in the sins of others.' The warning against hasty hand-laying is connected to appointment and ordination: rapid appointment of someone not sufficiently tested creates vicarious responsibility for the appointee's subsequent sins. The second clause — mēde koinōnei hamartiais allotriois — extends this: complicity in others' sins through endorsement (including premature ordination) makes one partially responsible for those sins. Seauton hagnon tērei (keep yourself pure) — the positive counterpart: personal purity is the foundation of discerning appointment.
23: mēketi hydropótei, alla oinō oligō chrō — 'no longer drink only water, but use a little wine for your stomach and your frequent illnesses.' The brief personal medical advice breaks from the public-ministry instructions to address Timothy's physical condition. The pastoral interruption has often puzzled commentators: why here? Possibly Timothy's asceticism (drinking only water) was being maintained out of deference to the false teachers' prohibitions (4:3); Paul's counter is practical and pastoral. Pyknais sou astheneiais (your frequent illnesses) — Timothy evidently had chronic health difficulties that the water-only regime was not alleviating.
24: tinōn anthrōpōn hai hamartiai prodēloi eisin proagousai eis krisin — 'the sins of some people are conspicuous, leading them forward to judgment.' The verse illustrates the difficulty of discerning fitness for ordination (v.22): some people's sins are visible in advance of any formal assessment (prodēloi — obvious beforehand, manifest already); others' sins are hidden and will only emerge later. The two categories require different evaluative approaches: the obvious disqualifies immediately; the hidden will eventually be revealed even if not now. The same principle applies to good deeds (v.25): some are visible, some are not, but they too cannot remain permanently hidden.
25: hōsautōs kai ta erga ta kala prodēla — 'likewise good deeds are conspicuous.' The structural parallel with v.24 applies the same visibility-logic to positive character evidence: some good works are immediately visible, others are not. Kai ta allōs echonta krubēnai ou dunantai (and even things that are otherwise cannot remain hidden forever) — the eschatological undercurrent: full disclosure is inevitable in the divine economy. For appointment purposes, this means both that hidden sins will eventually be revealed (warranting caution in appointment) and that hidden virtues will emerge over time (warranting patience in evaluation).
